title: This Is My Favorite Claude Code Feature...
summary: A YouTube video by NeuralNine reviewing a favorite feature of Claude Code, an AI coding assistant product. The creator discusses a specific capability they found particularly useful for coding work.
writeup: This video demonstrates Claude Code's remote control feature, which allows a running desktop Claude Code session to be driven from a mobile phone via a QR code or link. The host walks through activating remote control with the `/remote-control` slash command or the `--remote-control` CLI flag, shows prompts and tool-call approvals flowing from the phone in real time, and discusses practical limits: the feature suits low-stakes tasks like front-end tweaks, code-base exploration, and brainstorming, not deep engineering work. The through-line is that remote control turns idle transit time into productive asynchronous coding time, provided the session runs in an isolated sandbox.
key_points: Claude Code's remote control is activated either with the `/remote-control` slash command inside an interactive session or with the `--remote-control` CLI flag; omitting the double dashes (`claude remote control`) starts a phone-only session with no interactive terminal, which the host considers less useful.

From the phone, a user can send prompts and approve or deny tool calls without touching the laptop keyboard, demonstrated live by adding Flask routes and to-do app functionality purely from mobile input.

The `/exit` slash command can be sent from the phone to terminate the session, but the host notes it is unreliable enough that running the session inside a VM or isolated sandbox with restricted permissions is the safer production pattern.

Remote control is not suited for complex feature work requiring sustained focus; the host explicitly limits it to front-end style changes, asking about bugs or security issues, and exploring a code base conversationally.
Multiple projects can run simultaneous remote-control sessions, and the phone UI lists all of them so a user can switch between projects during the same commute or errand.
Voice input on the phone works as a prompt-entry method, making the feature usable hands-free during a walk.
